title: MTTR by Category
dataSources: Incident lifecycle data (open, assigned, resolved timestamps)
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" state="resolved"
| eval mttr_hours=round((resolved_at-opened_at)/3600,1)
| stats avg(mttr_hours) as avg_mttr, median(mttr_hours) as median_mttr by category
| sort -avg_mttr
description: MTTR per category identifies where process improvements or automation would have the greatest impact.
value: MTTR per category identifies where process improvements or automation would have the greatest impact.
implementation: Calculate MTTR from incident open to resolution timestamps. Break down by category, subcategory, and assignment group. Track trends over time. Set MTTR targets per category and report on achievement.
visualization: Bar chart (MTTR by category), Line chart (MTTR trend), Table (category MTTR summary), Histogram (resolution time distribution).
knownFalsePositives: MTTR and response drifts from harder incidents, new staff, on-call experience mix, or a wider scope of what we close as done.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_snow`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Incident lifecycle data (open, assigned, resolved timestamps).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Calculate MTTR from incident open to resolution timestamps. Break down by category, subcategory, and assignment group. Track trends over time. Set MTTR targets per category and report on achievement.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" state="resolved"
| eval mttr_hours=round((resolved_at-opened_at)/3600,1)
| stats avg(mttr_hours) as avg_mttr, median(mttr_hours) as median_mttr by category
| sort -avg_mttr
```

Understanding this SPL

**MTTR by Category** — MTTR per category identifies where process improvements or automation would have the greatest impact.

Documented **Data sources**: Incident lifecycle data (open, assigned, resolved timestamps). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_snow`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:incident. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:incident". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **mttr_hours** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by category** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ServiceNow list or form that matches this data (for example the incident, request, or problem view). Reconcile a few record numbers, states, and times. Confirm the add-on poll or export time lines up with what you see in the browser.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (MTTR by category), Line chart (MTTR trend), Table (category MTTR summary), Histogram (resolution time distribution).
grandmaExplanation: We measure how long it takes to fix different kinds of work, so we can see which areas drag and whether we are improving over time.
